CHARTS / BASKETS
% RETURN, REBASED · EOD 2026-06-12
Rotation ReadCitrini · claude

AI infrastructure is separating from the megacap platforms, and the spread is widening. The AI infra basket averaged +16.6% for the month with ARM leading. Semis added +14.3%, with MRVL in the top and NVDA lagging within its own basket. Cyber put up +14.1% with OKTA leading. The Mag 7 is down -8.1% for the month with AAPL the lone standout and AMZN the drag. The market is paying for the component and security layer. The platform layer is being distributed.

At the sector level, healthcare (XLV) leads at +5.45% for the month, financials (XLF) at +4.0%, and tech (XLK) at +2.97%. Communication services (XLC) is the worst sector at -4.06%, consumer discretionary (XLY) at -1.98%. Banks at +10.9% with WFC leading confirms credit conditions are not the headwind. These three vectors, quality cash flows in healthcare and banks alongside picks-and-shovels exposure in semiconductors and AI infrastructure, are the trade. Uranium is down -18.3% for the month. Gold miners are off -14.7%. Capital is moving out of hard-asset speculation and into earnings-driven technology infrastructure.

Momentum is confirming this as a trend with staying power. MTUM/SPY sits at a z-score of 3.56, in the top 1% of its yearly range. VLUE/SPY at z = 2.66 and SPHB/SPY at z = 2.4 are both in the top 1% of their yearly ranges. When value, momentum, and high beta all stretch at the same time, the 'value' on offer is semiconductors and financials with high-beta profiles. The factor labels have converged. EWT/SPY at z = 2.7 and EWY/SPY at z = 2.16 confirm the semiconductor wave is global, with Taiwan and South Korea pulling the same direction.

Picks-and-shovels AI layer rips while Mag 7 platforms are being distributed

BULL

AI infra at +16.6%, semis at +14.3%, and cyber at +14.1% for the month show the capex supercycle is pricing into the component layer with conviction. Banks at +10.9% confirm financial conditions remain open. The breadth behind this rotation is wider than the index suggests.

BEAR

Mag 7 down -8.1% for the month with XLC in the bottom 3% of its yearly range vs. SPY means the largest index weights are being actively distributed. If that selling accelerates, the index stalls regardless of what semiconductors do.

TELL

XLC/SPY at z = -2.86 (bottom 3% of its yearly range) is the ratio to watch. A stabilization there signals the rotation is broadening back to the platform layer. If it keeps falling, the index has a structural cap.

⚠ VLUE/SPY in the top 1% of its yearly range (z = **2.66**) signals value outperforming broadly vs MTUM/SPY in the top 1% of its yearly range (z = **3.56**) signals momentum outperforming broadly — Value and momentum typically rotate against each other. Both stretched to extremes at the same time means the value trade this cycle is semiconductor and financial stocks that already carry strong momentum. The factor definitions have converged. Trust the momentum signal: it is confirming the trend.
⚠ Healthcare (XLV) leads all sectors at **+5.45%** for the month, which reads as a defensive rotation vs SPHB/SPY at z = **2.4** (top 1% of its yearly range) signals high beta is outperforming, a risk-on posture — Healthcare is leading here on GLP-1 pipeline repricing and earnings beats, a growth dynamic in defensive packaging. Trust the SPHB/SPY read. The broader market posture is risk-on.

MTUM/SPY at z = 3.56, in the top 1% of its yearly range, is the sharpest signal in the table and it is a trend confirmation. Momentum is working because the right sectors are leading.

VLUE/SPY at z = 2.66 and SPHB/SPY at z = 2.4, both in the top 1% of their ranges, running alongside momentum at the same time confirms the 'value' this cycle is semiconductor and financial stocks that carry high-beta characteristics. XLC/SPY at z = -2.86 (bottom 3% of its range) and XLY/SPY at z = -2.24 (bottom 4%) look cheap on a relative-strength basis, but the distribution in Mag 7 and consumer weakness has no sign of turning.

EWT/SPY at z = 2.7 and EWY/SPY at z = 2.16 confirm Taiwan and South Korea are on the same semiconductor trade. QQQ/TLT at z = 2.27 tells you equities vs.

bonds remains extended: that ratio needs rates to cooperate or it compresses.

IWM/SPY +8.6% +2.0σMDY/SPY +4.8% +1.1σRSP/SPY +1.1% -0.2σIWM/QQQ +0.8% +0.1σIWB/SPY -0.1% -0.8σQQQE/QQQ -0.9% -0.7σIWO/IWN -2.3% -0.4σMDY/IWM -3.4% -1.3σRSP/QQQ -6.2% -1.5σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

Breadth ratios measure how widely gains spread across stocks: a rising line means the broader group is catching up to benchmarks, a falling line signals leadership concentrating in fewer positions. The month's leader is QQQE/QQQ at +4.47%, meaning equal-weight Nasdaq exposure is outpacing the cap-weighted index, a sign mid-size tech is closing the gap on the largest positions. IWM/SPY is the most stretched member at +2.03 sigma above its one-year mean, a short-burst push by small caps against large caps that sits well beyond normal.

The laggard, IWO/IWN (Russell 2000 Growth vs. Russell 2000 Value), is down -0.95% this month and -2.27% year-to-date, which tells you that within small caps investors are rotating toward cheaper value names and away from speculative growth. Two signals here pull in opposite directions: Nasdaq breadth is widening internally while small-cap quality preference is tightening against growth. If IWM/SPY mean-reverts from +2.03 sigma, treat it as the first sign the advance is narrowing back toward large-cap dominance. The data point that flips the read entirely is a sustained recovery in IWO/IWN; that would confirm small-cap risk appetite is genuinely expanding rather than rotating defensively.

VLUE/SPY +30.8% +2.7σSPHB/SPLV +18.2% +2.1σMTUM/SPY +17.2% +3.6σSPHB/SPY +14.2% +2.4σIVW/IVE +1.7% +0.8σSPYG/SPYV +1.6% +0.8σSIZE/SPY +0.3% -0.5σQUAL/SPY +0.2% -0.0σUSMV/SPY -5.5% -1.7σIWF/IWD -9.3% -1.5σMTUM/VLUE -10.5% -1.4σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

Factor ratios show how style premia perform against SPY or each other: a rising line means that factor is winning and a falling line means it is losing to the reference. Value is the dominant force right now: VLUE/SPY leads at +7.74% this month and +30.83% year-to-date, a gap large enough to force reallocation at the institutional level. IWF/IWD (large-cap growth vs. large-cap value) is the laggard at -5.01% on the month and -9.27% year-to-date, the clearest reflection of the style rotation underway.

MTUM/SPY is the most stretched member at 3.56 sigma above its one-year mean. Momentum is historically extended at the same time growth is losing badly to value: momentum strategies are still carrying winners from earlier in the year while fresh capital rotates elsewhere. An unwind from 3.56 sigma would hit the concentrated positions that drove year-to-date gains in mega-cap tech, and it would likely coincide with a further push in VLUE/SPY. The single data point that changes this regime is a re-acceleration in growth earnings that pulls IWF/IWD back above its one-year mean; until that arrives, the value rotation has the data on its side.

XLK/SPY +16.8% +2.7σXLE/SPY +16.0% +0.6σXLB/SPY +4.3% +0.1σXLRE/SPY +3.8% -0.5σXLI/SPY +3.2% +0.1σXLP/SPY +2.0% -0.8σXLU/SPY -4.7% -1.6σXLV/SPY -8.0% -0.7σXLY/SPY -9.0% -2.2σXLF/SPY -10.7% -1.4σXLC/SPY -11.3% -2.9σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

Each line in this basket is a sector ETF divided by SPY: a rising ratio means the sector is outperforming the market and a falling ratio means it is trailing. Healthcare leads the month with XLV/SPY at +6.01%, though it remains -7.96% behind SPY year-to-date, which means this month is a bounce off a badly lagging base rather than new structural leadership. XLC/SPY (Communication Services) is the laggard at -3.55% this month and -11.34% year-to-date, and the most stretched member at -2.86 sigma below its one-year mean.

XLC at -2.86 sigma is deep in the bottom of its yearly range against SPY. The absolute return context sharpens the read: Healthcare's absolute gain of +5.45% over the past month is the best of any sector, while XLC's -4.06% is the worst. Defensives leading on a relative basis while growth-oriented communication services collapses is a late-cycle rotation pattern. If XLC cannot reclaim its one-year mean against SPY over the next several weeks, size toward Healthcare, Staples, and Utilities; the defensive rotation is then confirmed by both ratio and absolute performance.

XLK/XLU +22.5% +2.5σXLE/XLU +21.7% +1.4σXLK/XLP +14.4% +1.9σXLI/XLU +8.3% +2.0σXLB/XLP +2.2% +1.1σXLI/XLP +1.2% +1.1σXLY/XLU -4.5% -0.4σXLF/XLU -6.3% -0.1σXLY/XLP -10.8% -0.9σXLF/XLP -12.4% -0.7σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

Cyclical-versus-defensive ratios show whether the market is pricing expansion (cyclicals leading, ratios rising) or contraction (defensives leading, ratios falling). XLF/XLU (Financials vs. Utilities) is the month's leader at +4.47% for the ratio, reflecting Financials' absolute return of +4.0% against Utilities' -0.45%, yet the ratio remains -6.26% below its year-start level. XLY/XLP (Consumer Discretionary vs. Consumer Staples) is the laggard at -3.07% this month and -10.83% year-to-date, reflecting Discretionary's absolute -1.98% against Staples' +1.12%.

XLK/XLU is the most stretched at 2.51 sigma above its one-year mean, a tech-over-utilities spread that sits well above normal. The split picture is this: high-multiple tech is still being bid while real consumer spending proxies are deteriorating. Financials recovering while Discretionary keeps losing to Staples gives you mixed economic signals. You are not seeing a broad cyclical re-acceleration; you are seeing pockets of rate-sensitive and momentum-driven outperformance inside a deteriorating consumer backdrop. The flip is a sustained reversal in XLY/XLP back toward its one-year mean, which requires a consumer catalyst or a sharp drop in staples that makes the ratio arithmetic work again.

EWY/SPY +76.3% +2.2σEWT/SPY +45.4% +2.7σEEM/EFA +11.3% +2.1σEEM/SPY +11.0% +1.8σEWJ/SPY +5.0% +0.4σEFA/SPY -0.3% -0.4σVWO/VEA -3.9% -1.4σEWG/EFA -8.3% -1.6σEWZ/EEM -9.1% -1.8σMCHI/SPY -18.5% -2.4σFXI/EEM -26.1% -2.0σINDA/EEM -26.1% -1.8σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

This basket plots international equity ETFs as ratios against SPY, so a rising line means that country or region is outperforming US large caps. EWT (Taiwan) vs SPY is the clear leader: +7.86% this month and +45.38% year-to-date, sitting 2.7 standard deviations above its one-year mean. FXI (China large caps) relative to EEM is the opposite: -8.03% this month and -26.06% year-to-date, dragging even within an already-weak EM index.

Taiwan's run is a semiconductor trade wearing a geography label. TSMC's position in AI chip manufacturing is the actual driver, and at 2.7 standard deviations extended you do not add here. The FXI/EEM gap (-26.06% YTD) confirms EM is splitting into two regimes: outside China, the index is attracting capital, while China absorbs outflows. Watch for EWT/SPY to start fading from these levels. If the fade comes on a TSMC earnings miss rather than a geopolitical headline, that changes the trade from avoiding the politics to evaluating supply-chain fundamentals directly.

SMH/XLK +28.8% +2.0σKRE/XLF +16.3% +1.8σOIH/XLE +15.1% +1.5σSOXX/SMH +14.4% +3.1σKBE/XLF +13.1% +1.8σXBI/XLV +10.4% +1.0σXHB/XLY +5.4% +0.3σXRT/XLY +4.4% +2.0σITB/XLY +2.2% -0.4σIBB/XLV +1.6% +0.4σXOP/XLE +1.3% -0.5σXME/XLB -1.0% +0.7σIGV/XLK -31.0% -1.8σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

This basket tracks sub-industry ETFs as ratios against their parent SPDR sectors, so a rising line means the sub-industry is beating the broader sector group it belongs to. XRT (retail) vs XLY (consumer discretionary) leads at +12.82% this month and +4.42% year-to-date. SOXX vs SMH is the most extended member at 3.14 standard deviations above its one-year mean, in the top 1% of its historical range. XBI (biotech) vs XLV (healthcare) is the laggard at -6.85% on the month despite a +10.38% year-to-date gain.

XRT outperforming XLY by +12.82% means the broad retail basket is working even as mega-cap consumer discretionary stocks weigh on the sector. SOXX/SMH at 3.14 standard deviations is the critical number: that extension almost always resolves by the lagging leg converging up rather than the leader selling off, so the trade is long SMH on any pullback. XBI losing -6.85% relative to XLV confirms healthcare rotation is flowing into large-cap pharma and managed care, not small biotech risk. When XBI/XLV starts recovering while SOXX/SMH pulls back, you have the setup for a healthcare small-cap rotation with semiconductor mean-reversion as the backdrop.

ETHA/IBIT -24.4% -1.3σGBTC/GLD -27.8% -1.1σIBIT/SPY -35.0% -1.6σBITO/SPY -36.3% -1.3σIBIT/QQQ -39.7% -1.7σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

This basket plots crypto ETFs as ratios against each other and against US equities, where a rising line means the first ticker is outperforming the second. Every ratio is negative on the month: ETHA/IBIT (Ethereum vs Bitcoin), the best performer in the group, is still -7.29% this month and -24.45% year-to-date. BITO/SPY (Bitcoin futures vs US equities) is the worst at -20.63% this month and -36.26% year-to-date. IBIT/QQQ sits -1.72 standard deviations below its one-year mean, in the bottom 5% of that range.

Every ratio negative means crypto is losing ground to equities across the board, and within crypto, Ethereum is losing ground to Bitcoin. BITO/SPY at -20.63% for the month means Bitcoin futures are being sold against US tech exposure. IBIT/QQQ at -1.72 standard deviations below its mean is the one potential mean-reversion setup in this basket, but you wait for absolute BTC price to stabilize before buying the ratio. The sequence to watch: IBIT/QQQ recovers first, ETHA/IBIT follows later. That order confirms Bitcoin is absorbing inflows before altcoins and sets up the entry.

XLK +26.2% +2.5σXLE +25.3% +1.3σXLB +12.7% +1.4σXLRE +12.2% +2.6σXLI +11.6% +1.7σXLP +10.3% +1.4σXLU +3.0% +0.4σXLV -0.5% +1.0σXLY -1.7% -0.0σXLF -3.5% +0.4σXLC -4.2% -0.4σ

This basket rebases all eleven SPDR sector ETFs to zero and tracks their paths from the same starting point: the spread between top and bottom shows where capital is actually moving. Healthcare (XLV) leads the past month at +5.45%, even though it carries the worst year-to-date return in the group at -0.52%. Communication services (XLC) is the double laggard: -4.06% this month and -4.17% year-to-date.

The YTD leaders, tech (XLK at +26.2%) and energy (XLE at +25.32%), are both fading on the month at +2.97% and -0.73% respectively. Capital is rotating into defensives: XLV and financials (XLF at +4.0% this month) are taking the bid. XLC is under pressure on both timeframes, and the selling looks structural rather than seasonal. You trim XLC and consumer discretionary (XLY at -1.67% YTD) and rotate into XLV and XLF where the one-month relative strength is building. The flip signal: XLK reaccelerates and pulls XLC with it, which would confirm the growth trade still has capital behind it.

GOOGL +14.2% +1.2σAAPL +9.0% +1.4σNVDA +8.0% +1.2σAMZN +4.8% +0.4σTSLA -8.6% +0.1σMETA -12.3% -1.8σMSFT -19.0% -1.6σ

The Mag 7 basket tracks seven mega-cap technology companies rebased to zero percent, so a rising line means the group is gaining as a unit. It is fracturing right now: AAPL leads at -1.18% for the month and +8.98% year-to-date, while AMZN has fallen -12.13% for the month and holds only +4.80% YTD.

AAPL at -1.18% and AMZN at -12.13% in the same month signal that the basket line is hiding more than it reveals. AMZN's monthly decline reflects a story change in investor expectations, and if it continues to weaken as AAPL softens, the aggregate rolls without warning. Size your exposure to individual stocks and watch whether AMZN stabilizes near current levels: a recovery there is what returns this group to functioning as a bull-market instrument.

The semis basket holds 11 stocks rebased to zero, with a rising line meaning semiconductor stocks are advancing together. Right now MRVL has surged +52.63% in one month and +203.85% year-to-date, while NVDA has pulled back -9.67% for the month and sits at only +8.02% YTD.

MRVL at +52.63% for the month and NVDA at -9.67% inside the same sector signals rotation. Capital is moving from AI-training silicon toward custom networking and inference chips, and the basket aggregate can still look constructive while the underlying story has already shifted. Whether NVDA finds a floor here is the key data point: sustained weakness in NVDA alongside MRVL's run means the rotation accelerates and exposure should sit in the networking layer rather than compute.

This basket tracks 12 stocks rebased to zero, and a rising line means the broader software universe is gaining as a group. The spread between leader and laggard is extreme: CRWD is up +22.32% for the month and +51.71% year-to-date, while ZS has fallen -20.95% in a month and -45.37% YTD.

CRWD gaining +22.32% and ZS losing -20.95% in the same month inside the same sector is earnings-driven stock selection. The group lift is absent. ZS at -45.37% YTD has been nearly halved while CRWD prints new highs, and the basket aggregate flatlines while individual outcomes are violent. Position in security spending specifics and avoid the multiple-compression stories until ZS or a similar stock shows a base at lower levels.

DDOG +72.9% +2.7σPANW +52.4% +2.8σCRWD +51.7% +2.8σNET +15.7% +1.2σMDB -11.9% +0.5σFDS -18.4% -0.8σSNOW -18.8% -0.8σPYPL -29.4% -1.6σNOW -32.7% -1.3σCRM -36.4% -1.9σWDAY -38.8% -1.4σADBE -40.9% -2.3σTEAM -43.9% -1.1σZS -45.4% -1.6σHUBS -53.7% -1.6σINTU -57.4% -2.0σ

The SaaS basket holds 16 stocks rebased to zero percent, and a rising line means cloud software companies are advancing together. The internals tell a different story: CRWD is up +22.32% in one month and +51.71% year-to-date, while INTU has fallen -27.85% for the month and -57.39% YTD.

INTU at -57.39% YTD has reset, which separates it from a standard drawdown. When the worst performer in a 16-stock basket is down more than half its starting value for the year, the basket-level chart is averaging a collapse against a rally and the aggregate line tells you nothing useful. CRWD is a security-spending story. INTU is a small-business and tax platform. Watch whether INTU's multiple compression spreads to other high-multiple SaaS stocks: if it does, the basket line turns lower even as CRWD holds.

IONQ +21.9% +0.8σQUBT -10.0% -0.7σRGTI -11.9% -0.1σQBTS -15.8% +0.2σARQQ -45.3% -1.2σ

The basket holds five quantum computing stocks rebased to zero, so a rising line means the group is gaining collectively and a falling line means the theme is in drawdown. Right now the group is fractured: RGTI leads at +12.92% for June but still sits -11.89% for the year, while QUBT drags at -10.15% for the month with a -9.99% YTD loss.

That gap between RGTI and QUBT in a single month tells you the market is stock-picking inside the theme rather than buying the quantum story wholesale. RGTI's bounce is real momentum, but YTD red across both leader and laggard means there is no sustained bid behind this group yet. You trade RGTI on its own merits with a tight stop. The read flips to a genuine theme trade if RGTI clears its -11.89% YTD hole and QUBT starts to participate on the upside.

The basket holds seven cybersecurity stocks rebased to zero, so a rising line means the group is gaining ground. OKTA exploded +47.38% in June and is +37.79% for the year, while ZS shed -20.95% this month and now sits -45.37% YTD.

OKTA at +47.38% and ZS at -20.95% in the same month is the market pricing individual earnings prints. A real sector rotation would have lifted both. OKTA's move rewards a specific result; ZS at -45.37% YTD reflects a re-rated growth story with no sign of reversal. If you hold the basket as a cybersecurity position, you are long OKTA and dragged by ZS. Size OKTA independently and drop the basket framing entirely.

The split between HOOD and COIN is the crypto correlation acting as a dividing line inside fintech. The market is paying for retail-brokerage exposure and selling pure-crypto infrastructure in the same month, so any recovery in this basket is stock-specific. HOOD's +19.82% June bounce against a -20.18% YTD loss puts it in recovery territory, but it has not reversed the year yet. The read changes if COIN stabilizes and crypto sentiment turns: at that point the entire basket reprices higher together.

The basket holds seven biotech and large-cap biopharma stocks rebased to zero. AMGN leads at +5.84% for the month and +8.69% YTD. REGN has lost -15.10% this month and sits -21.27% for the year.

AMGN's lead reflects the bid for earnings visibility over pipeline risk in this environment. REGN at -21.27% YTD is pricing competitive pressure and pipeline setbacks, and the distance between them tells you the market is buying quality within biotech and prioritizing cash flows over growth bets. You own the large-cap, cash-generating side and stay away from the speculative end of the space until a catalyst reopens the growth discount. The read flips if REGN prints positive Phase data and volume confirms accumulation above its recent lows.

A -20% single-month drawdown in KGC against a +4.48% leader tells you the basket is driven by company-specific events. The gold price is secondary here. You avoid using this sector group as a proxy for metal exposure. You watch for KGC stabilization and spread compression between leader and laggard as the first sign the stock-specific event is fading. The basket becomes a clean macro long only when that internal dispersion collapses.

The basket tracks 10 bank stocks rebased to 0%, where a rising line means the sector adds value in absolute terms. The month is uniformly positive: even JPM, the laggard, gained +5.74%, while WFC leads at +13.07%.

WFC's +13.07% monthly gain is paired with a year-to-date return of -12.67%, meaning you're watching an oversold recovery from a deep hole in 2026. JPM's -2.46% YTD tells you these two banks have been under different kinds of pressure all year. The most oversold bank in the basket is leading the month, and that's classic end-of-correction behavior in the sector. The flip signal: WFC gives back half its month's gains while XLF at +4.0% on the month rolls over.

MPC at +59.03% year-to-date while the sector ETF is down -0.73% on the month tells you refining margins are carrying these stocks while crude prices stall. WMB's monthly loss signals the market is rewarding barrels over pipelines right now. You take the refining angle in this basket and avoid midstream until crack spreads show life. The trade reverses when MPC's month-over-month lead over WMB starts to close.

The basket tracks 9 industrial stocks rebased to 0%, with XLI up +1.51% on the month as sector backdrop. GE leads at +13.7% for the month despite sitting at only +4.47% year-to-date, while ETN lags at -2.35% for the month though it retains +21.4% year-to-date.

GE's +13.7% monthly surge into a modest +4.47% YTD tells you the stock was badly underperforming the sector coming into June and is now catching up fast. ETN's +21.4% YTD alongside a negative month means the high-multiple electrification trade is pausing while defense and aerospace in GE catches a bid. You're watching a rotation within XLI from energy-transition plays toward cycle-sensitive hard industrials. If GE holds these gains, you add exposure in aerospace and defense over the electrification story.
